learning_objectives: Define serious games and distinguish them from entertainment games and gamification
Trace the historical evolution of serious games from military simulations to modern applications
Identify the major domains where serious games are deployed and their impact metrics
Analyze the tension between engagement and effectiveness in purpose-driven game design
readings: Serious Games: Mechanisms and Effects, Ch. 1-2 (Ritterfeld, Cody, Vorderer)
Bogost, Persuasive Games, Introduction

lecture_notes.introduction: Serious games apply game design principles to solve real-world problems. They educate, train, persuade, heal, and catalyze social change using the same mechanics that make entertainment games compelling. This is not a new idea -- military organizations have used war games for centuries, and flight simulators predate the commercial game industry. What is new is the scale and sophistication of modern serious games, which leverage advances in game technology, learning science, and behavioral psychology to achieve measurable outcomes. From FDA-approved digital therapeutics treating ADHD to corporate training simulations saving millions in reduced workplace accidents, serious games have moved from novelty to evidence-based practice. This course explores the theory, design, and evaluation of serious games and gamification systems. We examine what makes them effective (and what makes them fail), how to measure outcomes beyond fun, and how to design experiences that serve their serious purpose without sacrificing the engagement that makes games powerful. The central design challenge is balancing entertainment value with instructional or behavioral effectiveness -- too much fun and the purpose is lost, too much seriousness and no one plays.
key_concepts: Definition and taxonomy of serious games: educational, health, training, defense, social impact, advertising (advergames)
Historical roots: Kriegsspiel (1812), flight simulators (1920s), SIMNET military networked simulation (1980s), America's Army (2002)

Domains: education (K-12 and higher ed), health (rehabilitation, mental health, chronic disease), defense (tactical training, PTSD treatment), corporate (safety, compliance, leadership), social impact (empathy, civic engagement)
The tension between entertainment value and instructional effectiveness: the 'chocolate-covered broccoli' problem
Measuring outcomes beyond fun: learning gains, behavioral change, clinical outcomes, ROI
Serious game design frameworks: MDA (Mechanics, Dynamics, Aesthetics) applied to purpose-driven design
The evidence base: meta-analyses of serious game effectiveness across domains
Funding and business models: grants, corporate contracts, government procurement, direct-to-consumer
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analysis of three landmark serious games: America's Army (military recruitment and training), Re-Mission (pediatric cancer treatment adherence), and Foldit (citizen science protein folding) -- examining how each achieves its serious purpose through game mechanics
setup_requirements: Access to course LMS for discussion forums and reading materials
Steam or itch.io account for game analysis assignments
Serious game evaluation rubric template (provided on LMS)
Access to at least one serious game for hands-on analysis

review.value: Games designed with a primary purpose beyond entertainment, using game mechanics to achieve learning, behavioral, or social objectives while maintaining sufficient engagement to motivate voluntary play.

Balancing intrinsic motivation from gameplay with extrinsic instructional goals without undermining either. When the game is too fun, players skip the learning. When the learning is too prominent, players skip the game.
The pejorative term for serious games that simply wrap unengaging educational content in superficial game aesthetics (points, badges) without integrating learning into meaningful game mechanics.
Designing serious games based on empirical evidence of what works, validated through controlled studies and iterative testing with the target population.

learning_objectives: Distinguish gamification from serious game design and game-based learning
Apply the Octalysis framework to analyze and design gamified systems
Explain how self-determination theory underpins effective and ethical gamification
Identify the limitations and failure modes of superficial gamification
readings: Actionable Gamification, Ch. 1-5 (Yu-kai Chou)
Deci & Ryan, Self-Determination Theory overview paper

lecture_notes.introduction: Gamification adds game elements to non-game contexts to drive engagement and behavior change. When done well, it transforms tedious activities into motivating experiences -- language learning apps that feel like games, fitness trackers that create compelling progression systems, loyalty programs that turn routine purchases into rewarding quests. When done poorly, it slaps points and badges onto unchanged experiences and wonders why engagement spikes for two weeks then collapses. Understanding the psychology behind why game elements work is essential for designing systems that motivate rather than manipulate. Today we examine the Octalysis framework, which maps gamification mechanics to eight core drives of human motivation, and self-determination theory (SDT), which identifies autonomy, competence, and relatedness as the fundamental human needs that effective gamification must satisfy. We pay particular attention to the overjustification effect -- the well-documented phenomenon where adding external rewards for an activity that people already find intrinsically motivating can actually reduce their motivation. This is the trap that most corporate gamification falls into, and understanding it is the key to designing sustainable engagement systems.
key_concepts: Gamification vs game-based learning vs serious games: adding game elements vs using games for learning vs purpose-built games
Octalysis framework: eight core drives -- Epic Meaning, Accomplishment, Empowerment, Ownership, Social Influence, Scarcity, Unpredictability, Avoidance
Self-determination theory: autonomy (choice and control), competence (mastery and growth), relatedness (connection and belonging)
Intrinsic vs extrinsic motivation: doing something because it is inherently satisfying vs doing it for external rewards
The overjustification effect: external rewards undermining pre-existing intrinsic motivation
Points, badges, leaderboards (PBL) and their limitations: why these surface mechanics fail without deeper design
Gamification design process: identify target behavior -> understand user motivation -> select appropriate mechanics -> test and iterate
Long-term engagement: why novelty-dependent gamification fails and how to design for sustained motivation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Octalysis analysis of Duolingo's gamification system: mapping each feature (streaks, XP, leagues, hearts, gems) to core drives and evaluating which drives are over- or under-served
setup_requirements: Octalysis framework worksheet (provided on LMS)
Access to Duolingo free tier for hands-on exploration
SDT evaluation checklist for gamification auditing
Examples of gamified systems across domains for comparison

review.value: Epic Meaning, Accomplishment, Empowerment (creativity), Ownership, Social Influence, Scarcity, Unpredictability, Avoidance (loss aversion). Left-brain drives (2,4,6,8) are extrinsic; right-brain drives (1,3,5,7) are intrinsic.

When you add external rewards to an intrinsically motivating activity, people begin to attribute their motivation to the reward rather than the activity itself. Remove the reward and motivation drops below the pre-reward baseline.
Providing meaningful choices rather than prescribed paths. A gamification system that forces specific behaviors through reward schedules undermines autonomy and eventually generates resentment.
The superficial addition of points and badges without meaningful game design, resulting in short-term engagement spikes followed by rapid decline as the novelty wears off.

learning_objectives: Apply Bloom's taxonomy to educational game design objectives at multiple cognitive levels
Design game mechanics that scaffold learning progressions through zone of proximal development
Evaluate educational games using evidence-based criteria and research methodology
Implement stealth assessment that measures learning through gameplay actions
readings: Digital Games and Learning, Ch. 3-4 (Perrotta et al.)
Gee, What Video Games Have to Teach Us About Learning and Literacy, Ch. 2-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Educational games are most effective when learning objectives and game mechanics reinforce each other structurally rather than being layered on top of each other. A math game where you solve equations to earn coins for a decorative house is chocolate-covered broccoli. A math game where mathematical thinking is the core mechanic for solving puzzles that the player genuinely wants to solve is integrated design. The distinction matters enormously for learning outcomes. Research shows that games with integrated learning mechanics produce significantly better transfer of knowledge to real-world contexts than games with bolted-on quiz elements. Today we examine the learning science frameworks that guide effective educational game design: Bloom's taxonomy for defining what cognitive level the game targets, Vygotsky's zone of proximal development for calibrating challenge, constructivist learning theory for designing discovery-based mechanics, and stealth assessment for measuring learning without interrupting gameplay. We analyze published educational games through these lenses, identifying what makes DragonBox effective for algebra learning and Kerbal Space Program effective for teaching orbital mechanics -- neither game explicitly teaches, yet both produce measurable learning outcomes.
key_concepts: Bloom's taxonomy mapped to game mechanics: remember (matching), understand (simulation), apply (problem-solving), analyze (strategy), evaluate (optimization), create (sandbox)
Zone of proximal development and adaptive difficulty: keeping players in the productive struggle between too easy and too hard
Constructivist learning through exploration and experimentation: letting players discover principles rather than being told them
Stealth assessment: measuring player learning through gameplay actions and decisions rather than explicit test questions
Transfer of learning: designing so that skills practiced in-game transfer to real-world application contexts
Scaffolding: temporary supports that enable learners to perform beyond their current ability, gradually removed as competence grows
Embedded assessment vs bolted-on quizzes: integrating evaluation into gameplay flow vs interrupting play for testing
Evidence-based evaluation: pre/post testing, control group comparison, and effect size measurement for educational games
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative analysis of DragonBox (algebra) and Kerbal Space Program (orbital mechanics) as educational games: mapping their mechanics to Bloom's taxonomy and evaluating transfer potential
setup_requirements: Access to at least one educational game for hands-on evaluation
Learning objective mapping template (provided on LMS)
Bloom's taxonomy reference card with game mechanic examples
Stealth assessment design worksheet

review.value: Measuring player learning through gameplay actions and decisions rather than explicit test questions. The game observes what the player does and infers what they know from their behavioral patterns.

Gradually increasing challenge aligned with Vygotsky's zone of proximal development, keeping players in a flow state where they are learning through productive struggle rather than frustration or boredom.
When the game mechanic IS the learning activity (manipulating algebraic expressions in DragonBox) rather than the game being a reward for completing a separate learning activity.

Players may master a skill within the game context but fail to apply it outside the game. Effective educational games design for transfer by gradually increasing abstraction and real-world connection.

learning_objectives: Identify therapeutic goals addressable through game-based interventions across health domains
Analyze the design principles behind FDA-approved digital therapeutics
Evaluate clinical evidence for health game effectiveness using research methodology
Navigate regulatory pathways for digital therapeutic game development
readings: Games for Health Journal, selected papers on Re-Mission 2 and EndeavorRx
Thompson et al., Serious Video Games for Health, review article

lecture_notes.introduction: Health games represent one of the most rigorously validated categories of serious games. From physical rehabilitation to mental health treatment, games are being prescribed alongside traditional medicine based on clinical trial evidence. The landmark moment was EndeavorRx (now EndeavorOTC), the first game to receive FDA clearance as a prescription digital therapeutic for pediatric ADHD. This was not a gimmick -- it went through randomized controlled trials with published peer-reviewed results, the same evidence standard as pharmaceutical drugs. The game works by training selective attention through a mechanic where players navigate an avatar through visually complex environments, requiring them to focus on relevant stimuli while ignoring distractors -- essentially a continuous performance task wrapped in an engaging game. Today we examine health games across the spectrum: physical therapy games that motivate repetitive rehabilitation exercises, cognitive training games that target specific neural pathways, mental health games that provide therapeutic interventions for anxiety and depression, and chronic disease management games that improve medication adherence. For each category, we analyze the design principles that connect game mechanics to therapeutic mechanisms and evaluate the quality of the clinical evidence supporting their effectiveness.
key_concepts: Health game categories: physical therapy/rehabilitation, cognitive training, mental health (anxiety, depression, PTSD), chronic disease management, health education
FDA regulatory pathway for digital therapeutics: De Novo classification, 510(k) clearance, clinical trial requirements, post-market surveillance
EndeavorRx case study: game mechanic (selective attention training), clinical trials (STARS-ADHD study), and FDA clearance process
Clinical trial design for games: randomized controlled trials, active control vs waitlist control, outcome measures, follow-up duration
Patient adherence and engagement: why many health interventions fail because patients stop using them, and how game design solves this
Ethical considerations in health game design: informed consent, data privacy (HIPAA), avoiding over-reliance on games as treatment
Exergames: games requiring physical movement for rehabilitation (Wii Fit, Ring Fit Adventure) and their evidence base
Mental health applications: Sparx (CBT for depression), PTSD Coach, SuperBetter (resilience building) and their clinical evidence
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design review of EndeavorRx: examining how specific game mechanics target attention control pathways, how the difficulty adaptation system maintains therapeutic challenge, and how the clinical evidence supports the design decisions
setup_requirements: Access to published clinical trial data for health games (provided via LMS)
Health game evaluation rubric template
FDA digital therapeutic guidance documents (excerpts provided)
Comparative analysis worksheet for health game case studies

review.value: Software-based interventions that deliver evidence-based therapeutic treatments, reviewed and approved through regulatory pathways like the FDA. Distinct from wellness apps because they require clinical evidence of efficacy.

Game mechanics specifically designed to encourage sustained engagement with therapeutic content over weeks or months of treatment. Addresses the fundamental problem that most health interventions fail because patients stop using them.
In clinical trials, a comparison group that receives an equally engaging non-therapeutic game, controlling for the placebo effect of simply playing any game. More rigorous than waitlist control.

The correlation between how much a patient plays the therapeutic game and their clinical improvement. Establishing this relationship strengthens the evidence that the game mechanic, not just engagement, drives outcomes.

learning_objectives: Design simulation-based training that demonstrably transfers to real-world job performance
Apply Kirkpatrick's four levels to measure gamification and training ROI
Identify common failure modes in corporate gamification and how to avoid them
Build business cases for game-based training using cost-benefit analysis
readings: The Gamification of Learning and Instruction, Ch. 5-7 (Kapp)
Harvard Business Review, 'Gamification at Work' case studies

lecture_notes.introduction: Corporate training and simulation represent the largest market segment for serious games by revenue. The global game-based learning market exceeds $15 billion annually, driven by enterprises seeking more effective and cost-efficient training alternatives. The business case is straightforward: traditional e-learning has completion rates of 15-20%, while game-based training achieves 60-80% completion with significantly higher knowledge retention measured 30 days later. Walmart reported a 10% increase in employee satisfaction and significantly improved customer safety metrics after deploying VR training for active-shooter scenarios, slip-and-fall prevention, and new technology rollouts. But for every Walmart success story, there are dozens of failed corporate gamification initiatives that wasted budget and generated employee cynicism. The most common failure mode is what we call pointsification -- slapping points and leaderboards onto unchanged training content and expecting magical engagement. Effective corporate game-based training requires the same rigorous design process as any serious game: clearly defined performance objectives, mechanics that practice the target skills, assessment that measures real transfer, and evaluation that proves ROI. Today we build the analytical framework for designing and measuring corporate game-based training using Kirkpatrick's four levels of evaluation.
key_concepts: Simulation fidelity spectrum: low-fidelity scenarios (text-based branching) to full virtual environments (VR safety training)
Kirkpatrick's four levels: Reaction (did they like it), Learning (did they learn), Behavior (did they apply it), Results (did it impact the organization)
Engagement metrics for training: completion rates, time-on-task, voluntary replay, performance improvement over sessions
Common gamification anti-patterns: pointsification, chocolate-covered broccoli, mandatory fun, surveillance disguised as gamification
Case studies: Walmart VR safety training, Deloitte Leadership Academy, McDonald's Till Training, Duolingo for enterprise
ROI calculation: cost of development and deployment vs savings from reduced errors, faster onboarding, lower turnover
Compliance training gamification: making mandatory training engaging without trivializing serious content
Simulation transfer: the relationship between simulation fidelity, practice conditions, and real-world performance improvement
lecture_notes.demonstration: ROI analysis of a corporate gamification program using Kirkpatrick's model: comparing traditional e-learning to game-based training across all four levels with real cost and outcome data
setup_requirements: Spreadsheet software for ROI calculations
Access to sample training effectiveness data (provided on LMS)
Kirkpatrick evaluation template
Corporate gamification design checklist

review.value: Level 1 Reaction: did they enjoy it? Level 2 Learning: did they gain knowledge/skills? Level 3 Behavior: did they apply it on the job? Level 4 Results: did it improve organizational outcomes? Most evaluations stop at Level 1-2; Levels 3-4 demonstrate real impact.

The superficial addition of points and badges without meaningful game design, resulting in short-term engagement spikes followed by rapid decline. The game industry equivalent of putting a racing stripe on a slow car.

Training is most effective when practice conditions match the conditions of actual performance. A sales training simulation should present realistic customer scenarios, not abstract quiz questions about sales methodology.

Making compliance training too game-like risks trivializing serious content (workplace harassment, safety procedures). The gamification must enhance engagement without undermining the gravity of the subject.

class_schedule.topic: Social Impact Games and Persuasive Design
learning_objectives: Design games that drive measurable social awareness and behavioral change
Apply Bogost's procedural rhetoric framework to create persuasive game arguments
Evaluate the ethical boundaries of persuasive game design
Measure social impact using mixed-methods research approaches
readings: Bogost, Persuasive Games, Ch. 2-4
Games for Change research reports (selected publications)

lecture_notes.introduction: Games can shift perspectives, build empathy, and catalyze social action in ways that other media cannot. A documentary about poverty tells you about hardship. A game like Spent forces you to make the impossible choices that poverty demands -- pay rent or buy medicine, eat healthy or feed everyone -- and the experience of those choices creates understanding that passive viewing cannot match. Ian Bogost calls this procedural rhetoric: the practice of making arguments through the rules and mechanics of a game rather than through narrative or visual content alone. When you play Papers, Please and must decide between following unjust immigration rules to feed your family or helping refugees at personal cost, the rules themselves are the argument. The game does not tell you immigration enforcement is morally complex -- it makes you experience the complexity through meaningful choices with consequences. Today we examine games designed for social impact across domains: poverty awareness, climate change action, refugee empathy, democratic participation, and public health. For each, we analyze how game mechanics create persuasive arguments, how impact is measured, and where the ethical line falls between persuasion and manipulation. Persuasive games are powerful precisely because they engage players emotionally and experientially, which creates a responsibility to use that power honestly.
key_concepts: Procedural rhetoric: making arguments through game rules and mechanics rather than through narrative content alone
Empathy games and perspective-taking design: putting players in others' shoes through role-playing and constrained choice
Social impact categories: awareness (changing what people know), attitude (changing what people believe), behavior (changing what people do)
Impact measurement: pre/post surveys for attitude change, behavioral tracking for action, longitudinal studies for lasting impact
Games for Change movement: the community and annual festival dedicated to social impact games
Case studies: Papers Please (immigration ethics), Spent (poverty simulation), That Dragon Cancer (grief empathy), Parable of the Polygons (segregation dynamics)
Ethical boundaries of persuasive design: transparency about intent, avoiding emotional manipulation, respecting player autonomy
Funding models for impact games: grants (NEA, NEH, foundations), impact investment, government contracts, educational licensing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Play and analysis of Papers, Please and Spent: mapping how game mechanics create persuasive arguments about immigration and poverty through procedural rhetoric
setup_requirements: Access to Papers, Please (Steam) and Spent (browser-based, free)
Procedural rhetoric analysis worksheet
Impact measurement framework template
Ethics evaluation checklist for persuasive design

review.value: The practice of making arguments through the rules and mechanics of a game. Papers, Please argues that immigration enforcement creates moral dilemmas not through dialogue but through rules that force players to choose between duty and compassion.

Perspective-taking that goes beyond narrative ('imagine being poor') to experiential ('make these impossible tradeoffs with limited resources'). The constrained choice space creates emotional understanding.

Many social impact games successfully change awareness and attitudes but fail to translate that into real-world behavior change. Closing this gap requires explicit action pathways connected to the game experience.

Persuasion presents an argument that the audience can evaluate and accept or reject. Manipulation bypasses critical evaluation through emotional exploitation. Serious game designers must ensure their games persuade rather than manipulate.

learning_objectives: Apply comprehensive accessibility guidelines to serious game design
Design for diverse abilities across motor, visual, auditory, and cognitive dimensions
Ensure accessibility accommodations do not reduce the serious purpose effectiveness
Conduct accessibility audits using standardized evaluation frameworks
readings: Game Accessibility Guidelines (gameaccessibilityguidelines.com), complete guidelines
Inclusive Design for Serious Games (journal article, provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Accessibility in serious games carries double stakes. An inaccessible entertainment game excludes players from fun. An inaccessible serious game excludes people from education, training, therapy, or social participation -- outcomes that may significantly affect their lives. If your health game cannot be played by someone with the very condition it treats, it fails its core purpose. If your corporate training game excludes employees with disabilities, it creates legal liability under the ADA and fails to train your full workforce. Accessibility is not an add-on feature or a compliance checkbox -- it is a fundamental design requirement that must be integrated from the earliest concept stage. Today we examine accessibility across four dimensions: motor (can players with limited dexterity or mobility interact with the game?), visual (can players with low vision, blindness, or color deficiency perceive the game?), auditory (can deaf or hard-of-hearing players access all information?), and cognitive (can players with learning disabilities, attention differences, or cognitive impairments engage with the game?). For each dimension, we identify specific design patterns that enable access without reducing the serious purpose of the game. This is the critical constraint: accessibility accommodations must maintain the learning, training, or therapeutic effectiveness. A cognitive accessibility mode that skips the challenging parts of a therapeutic game undermines the therapy.
key_concepts: Motor accessibility: remappable controls, switch access, one-handed play options, adjustable timing requirements, eye tracking support
Visual accessibility: screen reader support, high-contrast modes, scalable UI, colorblind-safe palettes, audio descriptions for visual information
Auditory accessibility: subtitles and captions, visual sound indicators, haptic feedback alternatives, sign language video options
Cognitive accessibility: adjustable complexity, clear navigation, consistent interface patterns, reduced sensory overload options, save-anywhere
Preserving serious purpose: ensuring that accessibility accommodations maintain the effectiveness of learning, training, or therapeutic mechanics
Universal design vs accommodation: building access into the core design rather than adding alternate paths that feel inferior
Accessibility audit methodology: systematic evaluation checklist, user testing with diverse ability groups, WCAG compliance mapping
Legal requirements: ADA (US), Equality Act (UK), EN 301 549 (EU), Section 508 (US federal), and their application to digital games
lecture_notes.demonstration: Accessibility audit of a published serious game using the game accessibility guidelines checklist: identifying barriers across all four dimensions and proposing specific design modifications
setup_requirements: Game accessibility guidelines checklist (printed and digital)
Screen reader software (NVDA or VoiceOver) for testing
Color blindness simulation tools
Published serious game for hands-on audit exercise

review.value: Designing products usable by all people to the greatest extent possible without adaptation. In games: core mechanics that work across ability levels rather than requiring separate 'accessible' modes.
Accessibility accommodations must not bypass the serious purpose. A cognitive accessibility mode in a training game should simplify the interface, not skip the training scenarios.

Playtesting with participants who have the disabilities your accommodations target. Simulating disability (blindfolding sighted testers) is a poor substitute for testing with people who actually navigate the world with that disability.

People with disabilities are disproportionately represented in the populations that serious games serve (patients, workers needing accommodation, students with learning differences). Inaccessible serious games fail the people who need them most.

learning_objectives: Apply behavior change theories (Fogg, Transtheoretical) to gamified intervention design
Design game mechanics that target specific stages of behavior change readiness
Evaluate the sustainability of game-driven behavior change over time
Distinguish between behavior change, attitude change, and knowledge acquisition as design goals
readings: BJ Fogg, Tiny Habits, Ch. 1-3
Prochaska, Transtheoretical Model of Behavior Change (overview paper)

lecture_notes.introduction: The ultimate goal of many serious games and gamification systems is to change behavior in the real world: exercising more, eating healthier, studying consistently, practicing safety procedures, reducing energy consumption. But behavior change is among the hardest outcomes to achieve and sustain. Most people who join a gym stop going within three months. Most people who install a health app abandon it within two weeks. The game designer working on behavior change must understand why behavior change is so difficult and design systems that address the specific psychological barriers at each stage of the change process. Today we examine two complementary frameworks: BJ Fogg's behavior model (Behavior = Motivation + Ability + Prompt) and the Transtheoretical model (stages of change: precontemplation, contemplation, preparation, action, maintenance). Together, these frameworks explain when and why game mechanics can effectively promote behavior change, and when they are likely to fail. The key insight is that different mechanics are needed at different stages. A person who has not yet decided to exercise (precontemplation) needs awareness-building mechanics. A person who exercises regularly but is at risk of relapse (maintenance) needs social accountability mechanics. One-size-fits-all gamification ignores these stages and fails.
key_concepts: Fogg Behavior Model: Behavior = Motivation (M) + Ability (A) + Prompt (P), all three must be present simultaneously
Transtheoretical model stages: precontemplation (unaware), contemplation (considering), preparation (planning), action (doing), maintenance (sustaining)
Stage-matched mechanics: awareness content for precontemplation, goal-setting for preparation, tracking for action, social support for maintenance
Habit formation through games: cue-routine-reward loops, implementation intentions, and the role of variable rewards
Sustainability challenge: what happens when the game is removed? Designing for internalized motivation rather than game-dependent behavior
Relapse prevention: game mechanics that support recovery from setbacks rather than punishing failure
Measurement: behavioral outcome tracking, follow-up studies at 30/60/90 days post-intervention, regression analysis of sustaining factors
Ethical boundaries: the difference between supporting autonomous behavior change and using persuasive technology to impose behavior change
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a behavior change gamification system for a specific target behavior (e.g., consistent medication adherence) using stage-matched mechanics: mapping each Transtheoretical stage to appropriate game elements
setup_requirements: Behavior change design canvas template (provided on LMS)
Fogg Behavior Model worksheet
Transtheoretical Model stage mapping tool
Case study examples of behavior change games (provided)

review.value: Behavior occurs when Motivation, Ability, and a Prompt converge simultaneously. Games can increase motivation (rewards), reduce ability barriers (simplification), and provide prompts (notifications). All three must be addressed.
Different behavior change stages require different mechanics. A leaderboard (maintenance stage) is counterproductive for someone in precontemplation who has not yet decided to change.

If players only exercise because the game rewards them, they will stop when the game is removed. Effective design gradually shifts motivation from extrinsic game rewards to intrinsic satisfaction with the behavior.

Most behavior change involves setbacks. Games that punish missed days (breaking a streak destroys progress) can trigger complete abandonment. Better: acknowledge setbacks, reduce the restart barrier, celebrate recovery.

class_schedule.topic: Research Methods for Serious Game Evaluation
learning_objectives: Design rigorous research studies to evaluate serious game effectiveness
Select appropriate research methods (experimental, quasi-experimental, mixed methods) for different evaluation questions
Analyze and interpret evaluation data using statistical and qualitative methods
Write evaluation reports that meet academic publication and grant reporting standards
readings: Research Methods in Game Studies (selected chapters, provided on LMS)
Clark, Tanner-Smith, Killingsworth: Digital Games, Design, and Learning meta-analysis

lecture_notes.introduction: Serious games must prove they work. Unlike entertainment games where commercial success is the ultimate metric, serious games are judged by their measurable impact on learning, health, behavior, or social outcomes. This requires rigorous evaluation using research methods borrowed from education, psychology, and clinical science. Today we learn to design evaluation studies that produce credible evidence. We cover the hierarchy of evidence from case studies (weakest) through randomized controlled trials (strongest), the practical constraints that make ideal study designs impossible in real-world settings, and the mixed-methods approaches that combine quantitative outcome measurement with qualitative understanding of player experience. We also confront the publication and reporting bias that plagues the serious games literature: studies that find positive results are more likely to be published, creating an inflated sense of effectiveness. Critical evaluation of existing research is as important as conducting new research. By the end of this session, you will be able to design an evaluation study for your own serious game project, collect meaningful data, analyze it appropriately, and report findings honestly.
key_concepts: Research design hierarchy: randomized controlled trial (gold standard), quasi-experimental (pre/post with comparison group), single-group pre/post, case study
Control conditions: no-treatment control, active control (alternative activity), attention control (non-therapeutic game), waitlist control
Outcome measures: standardized tests, behavioral observation, self-report surveys, physiological measures, game analytics telemetry
Mixed methods: combining quantitative outcome measures with qualitative player interviews, observations, and think-aloud protocols
Sample size and statistical power: how many participants you need to detect a meaningful effect
Effect size reporting: Cohen's d and its interpretation (0.2 = small, 0.5 = medium, 0.8 = large) for communicating practical significance
Common threats to validity: selection bias, attrition, novelty effects, experimenter effects, Hawthorne effect
Publication bias: the tendency to publish positive results disproportionately, and how to account for it when reviewing evidence
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a complete evaluation study for a hypothetical educational game: defining research questions, selecting measures, calculating required sample size, planning data collection, and specifying the analysis plan
setup_requirements: Research design template (provided on LMS)
G*Power software for sample size calculation (free)
Statistical analysis reference sheet (provided)
Example evaluation reports from published serious game studies

review.value: A comparison group that receives an equally engaging but non-purposive activity. Controls for the possibility that any engaging activity (not just the specific serious game) produces the measured outcome.

A standardized measure of how large the difference between treatment and control groups is. More informative than p-values because it tells you the practical magnitude, not just statistical significance.
Initial improvement caused by the newness of the intervention rather than its specific design. Controlled by running studies long enough for novelty to wear off (typically 4+ weeks).
Quantitative measures tell you whether the game works. Qualitative methods tell you how and why it works (or fails), providing design insights that numbers alone cannot reveal.

class_schedule.topic: Gamification in Civic Engagement and Democracy
learning_objectives: Design gamified systems that encourage civic participation and democratic engagement
Evaluate the effectiveness and risks of gamifying political and civic processes
Analyze case studies of civic engagement games and platforms
Address the ethical concerns unique to gamifying democracy
readings: Civic Games reader (provided compilation of research articles)
Bogost, Persuasive Games, Ch. 5 (Political Games)

lecture_notes.introduction: Can game design save democracy? That is a provocative question, but serious games and gamification are increasingly being applied to civic challenges: voter engagement, participatory budgeting, community planning, civic education, and government transparency. Some applications are straightforward and uncontroversial -- a civics education game that teaches students how government works. Others sit on the ethical edge -- a gamified voting system that rewards participation with points and badges. Today we examine the full spectrum of civic engagement games, from educational simulations that help citizens understand policy trade-offs to participatory platforms that let communities make real decisions through game-like interfaces. We also confront the serious ethical concerns: gamifying voting could trivialize the democratic process, persuasive political games can be propaganda dressed in interactive clothing, and gamified civic platforms can be captured by organized interest groups who game the system. These concerns do not invalidate the field but they demand careful, transparent design with built-in safeguards. The potential reward is significant: increasing civic participation among demographics (young voters, marginalized communities) that traditional political engagement has failed to reach.
key_concepts: Civic education games: simulations that teach government processes, policy trade-offs, and democratic principles
Participatory budgeting platforms: gamified interfaces that let communities allocate real public funds through deliberative processes
Voter engagement: gamified reminders, social accountability, and information delivery systems (not gamifying the vote itself)
Government transparency games: interactive visualizations that make public data accessible and engaging
Political simulation games: games that model political systems to build understanding of governance complexity
Ethics of civic gamification: avoiding trivialization, preventing manipulation, ensuring equitable access, maintaining democratic integrity
Case studies: iCivics (civic education), Decide Madrid (participatory budgeting), Budget Hero (fiscal policy), PeaceMaker (Israeli-Palestinian conflict)
Digital divide considerations: ensuring civic gamification does not further exclude populations without technology access
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing iCivics game suite: mapping how specific game mechanics teach constitutional principles, evaluating evidence of civic knowledge gains, and identifying design elements that could be improved
setup_requirements: Access to iCivics games (free, browser-based)
Civic engagement evaluation framework (provided on LMS)
Ethics analysis worksheet for civic gamification
Case study materials on participatory budgeting platforms

review.value: A democratic process where community members directly decide how to spend a portion of public funds. Gamified platforms can make this process more accessible and engaging, but must prevent gaming by organized interests.

Gamifying civic engagement risks reducing serious democratic participation to a game-like activity. Voting is not a quest, and policy decisions are not levels to complete. The gamification must enhance engagement without trivializing stakes.

Gamified civic platforms are only accessible to people with technology. If civic decisions move to digital platforms, populations without internet access are further excluded from democratic participation.

Democracy requires deliberation (considering multiple perspectives to reach consensus). Gamification often creates competition (winning vs losing). Civic gamification must promote deliberative rather than competitive dynamics.

learning_objectives: Design analytics frameworks that measure gamification effectiveness against defined objectives
Interpret engagement metrics to distinguish genuine motivation from superficial compliance
Apply iterative design methods to improve gamification systems based on user data
Identify when gamification is failing and make evidence-based decisions to pivot or remove it
readings: Measuring Gamification Effectiveness (journal article, provided on LMS)
Lean Analytics applied to gamification (provided reading)

lecture_notes.introduction: Building a gamification system is only half the work. Measuring whether it actually achieves its purpose, iterating based on evidence, and having the discipline to remove gamification that is not working are equally critical skills. Too many organizations deploy gamification, see initial engagement spikes, declare victory, and never measure long-term outcomes. When the spike inevitably fades, they blame the users rather than the design. Rigorous analytics prevent this by distinguishing between genuine behavior change (users internalized the target behavior) and superficial compliance (users perform the behavior only when monitored and rewarded). Today we build analytics frameworks specifically for gamification evaluation. We define leading indicators (early signals that predict long-term success) and lagging indicators (actual outcomes that take time to measure). We learn to identify gamification failure patterns in data: the novelty spike and decline curve, the gaming-the-system pattern where users optimize for points rather than the intended behavior, and the disengagement cliff where users suddenly stop participating. For each failure pattern, we discuss diagnostic approaches and design modifications that address the root cause.
key_concepts: Gamification analytics framework: awareness metrics, engagement metrics, behavior metrics, outcome metrics in a progressive funnel
Leading vs lagging indicators: daily active participation (leading) vs quarterly skill assessment scores (lagging)
Genuine engagement vs compliance: voluntary participation beyond requirements indicates genuine motivation; exact-minimum participation indicates compliance
Novelty effect curve: initial spike followed by decline to steady state -- the steady state reveals true impact, not the spike
Gaming-the-system detection: users optimizing for points/badges rather than the intended behavior (e.g., clicking through training without reading)
A/B testing gamification elements: isolating the contribution of specific mechanics (does removing the leaderboard affect outcomes?)
Iterative design cycle: measure -> analyze -> hypothesize -> modify -> test -> measure, applied to gamification specifically
When to remove gamification: evidence-based criteria for deciding that gamification is not working and should be eliminated or replaced
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a gamification system's analytics data to diagnose a novelty-decline pattern: identifying when the decline began, which user segments declined most, and proposing evidence-based modifications to the system design
setup_requirements: Sample gamification analytics dataset (provided on LMS)
Analytics framework template with metric definitions
Python or Excel for trend analysis and visualization
Failure pattern identification checklist

review.value: A sharp engagement spike in weeks 1-2 followed by steady decline. If the metric returns to pre-gamification baseline, the gamification only produced temporary novelty, not lasting change.

When users optimize for gamification rewards rather than the intended behavior. Detected by comparing point-earning patterns with actual outcome measures. If points go up but outcomes do not, users are gaming.
When users do more than required without additional reward, it indicates genuine motivation. This is the strongest signal that gamification has successfully fostered intrinsic engagement.

Remove gamification when: (1) engagement has returned to pre-gamification baseline, (2) users are gaming the system, (3) the cost of maintaining the system exceeds the value of the outcomes it produces, or (4) user feedback indicates resentment.

learning_objectives: Plan a serious game development project from concept through evaluation
Manage stakeholder relationships between subject matter experts, designers, and audiences
Apply rapid prototyping methods adapted for serious game validation
Navigate the funding and procurement landscape for serious game projects
readings: Serious Game Design and Development (Cannon-Bowers & Bowers), Ch. 6-8
Grant Writing for Games-Based Research (provided guide)

lecture_notes.introduction: Developing a serious game differs from developing an entertainment game in several critical ways that affect every phase of the project. You have stakeholders who are not gamers -- doctors, teachers, corporate trainers, government officials -- who must approve design decisions they may not intuitively understand. You have subject matter constraints that limit your creative freedom -- a medical game cannot simplify anatomy beyond a certain point without compromising therapeutic validity. You have evaluation requirements built into the project from the start -- the game must be designed to be measurable, which affects mechanics, data collection, and the entire research protocol. Today we map out the complete serious game development process from concept through evaluation and deployment. We identify the unique challenges at each phase: securing funding from organizations that may not understand game development, managing subject matter expert relationships without letting either the SME or the designer dominate, prototyping and validating quickly when your target audience may be difficult to access (hospitalized children, corporate executives, active military), and navigating procurement processes that were designed for buying software licenses, not commissioning interactive experiences.
key_concepts: Stakeholder management: balancing game designer vision, subject matter expert accuracy, funder requirements, and user needs
Subject matter expert collaboration: extracting knowledge without letting the SME overdesign, maintaining scientific/pedagogical accuracy
Rapid prototyping for serious games: paper prototypes, Wizard of Oz testing, minimum viable game for concept validation
Funding landscape: government grants (NSF, NIH, DoE, DoD), foundation grants, corporate sponsorship, impact investment
Grant writing for games: translating game design into research methodology language that reviewers understand
Institutional Review Board (IRB) requirements: ethical approval for research involving human participants in playtesting
Deployment contexts: classroom integration, clinical settings, corporate LMS platforms, public access web/mobile
Sustainability planning: maintaining and updating the game after the initial funding period ends
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop: developing a project plan for a hypothetical serious game including stakeholder map, development timeline, evaluation design, and funding strategy
setup_requirements: Project planning template with serious game-specific sections
Stakeholder mapping exercise worksheet
Grant opportunity database access (NIH Reporter, NSF Award Search)
Sample IRB protocol for game-based research

review.value: Subject matter experts provide accuracy; game designers provide engagement. Neither should dominate. The ideal process: SME defines learning objectives and accuracy constraints, designer proposes mechanics, both iterate together.

Any research involving human participants (including playtesting with the intent to publish findings) requires Institutional Review Board approval. Plan for 2-3 months of review time in your project timeline.
The smallest playable version that tests the core hypothesis: does this mechanic teach this concept? Build and test the core interaction before investing in full production.
Grant funding pays for development but not ongoing maintenance. Plan from day one for who will host, update, and support the game after the grant period ends.

learning_objectives: Evaluate VR, AR, and mixed reality platforms for serious game applications
Assess the added value and costs of immersive technology for specific serious game domains
Design serious game experiences for emerging platforms with appropriate fidelity
Analyze the current evidence base for immersive serious games
readings: Virtual Reality in Education and Training (journal review, provided on LMS)
Meta Quest Developer Documentation for Education (selected sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: Virtual reality, augmented reality, and mixed reality offer compelling new possibilities for serious games -- and some significant new challenges. VR excels at creating presence, the feeling of being physically present in a virtual environment, which makes it powerful for situations where real-world training is dangerous (fire safety, surgery, combat), expensive (heavy equipment operation, rare scenario exposure), or impossible (historical reenactment, space exploration). AR overlays game elements onto the real world, creating possibilities for location-based learning, on-the-job training overlays, and physical therapy games that use real objects. But immersive technology also introduces barriers: VR headsets are expensive, cause motion sickness in some users, require dedicated space, and add development complexity. The critical question for serious game designers is not 'can we make this in VR?' but 'does VR add enough value to justify the additional cost, complexity, and accessibility barriers?' Today we evaluate immersive technology through this lens, examining the evidence for when VR/AR adds measurable value to serious game outcomes and when traditional platforms achieve equivalent results at lower cost.
key_concepts: Presence: the psychological feeling of being physically located in a virtual environment, unique to VR, and its impact on learning and therapy
VR serious game domains with strong evidence: surgical training, phobia exposure therapy, empathy experiences, hazardous environment training
AR serious game applications: on-the-job training overlays, location-based education, physical rehabilitation, collaborative learning
Cost-benefit analysis: when immersive technology adds measurable value vs when it adds complexity without improving outcomes
Accessibility challenges: motion sickness, visual accommodation, cost barriers, hygiene concerns, physical space requirements
Design considerations: locomotion methods, interaction paradigms, session length limits (20-30 min for comfort), onboarding for VR-naive users
AI integration: adaptive AI tutors in serious games, procedural content generation for training scenarios, natural language interaction
The metaverse question: persistent shared virtual spaces for education and training -- promise, hype, and current reality
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative analysis of a VR surgical training simulator vs a desktop version: examining the evidence for presence-based learning advantages and evaluating whether the VR version's additional costs are justified by outcome improvements
setup_requirements: VR headset (Meta Quest or equivalent) for hands-on demonstration
Published VR vs desktop comparison studies (provided on LMS)
Cost-benefit analysis template for immersive technology decisions
AR development toolkit overview (ARKit/ARCore documentation excerpts)

review.value: VR creates a sense of physical presence in the learning environment that can improve embodied learning (surgical skills, spatial understanding) but may not improve cognitive learning (facts, concepts) compared to desktop.

Conflict between visual and vestibular systems causes nausea in 40-70% of users during locomotion in VR. Serious game designers must account for this with comfort settings and teleport-based movement.
Does the VR/AR version produce measurably better outcomes than a well-designed desktop version? If not, the additional cost and accessibility barriers are not justified.

Integrating AI systems that adapt to individual learner needs in real time: adjusting difficulty, providing hints, detecting misconceptions, and personalizing the learning path within the serious game.

class_schedule.topic: Serious Game Portfolio and Professional Development
learning_objectives: Build a portfolio that demonstrates serious game design skills to employers and grant funders
Navigate the serious games industry landscape including career paths and organizations
Write grant proposals and project pitches for serious game development
Connect course learning to emerging opportunities in the serious games field
readings: Serious Games Industry Report (provided on LMS)
Career Paths in Serious Games and Gamification (provided reading)

lecture_notes.introduction: The serious games industry spans education technology, health technology, defense contracting, corporate training, and social impact organizations. Career paths include game designer, learning experience designer, gamification consultant, research scientist, product manager for educational software, and UX designer for health applications. Unlike the entertainment game industry, serious games professionals often work at the intersection of game design and another domain -- a health game designer needs to understand both game mechanics and clinical research methodology. Today we focus on preparing you for this interdisciplinary career. We discuss portfolio construction that demonstrates both design skills and domain understanding, the organizations and conferences where serious games professionals connect (Games for Change, Serious Play Conference, GDC Serious Games Summit, Games for Health Europe), and the funding mechanisms that drive the field. We also practice the most important professional skill for serious games: the ability to communicate the value of games to non-game stakeholders -- administrators, funders, clinicians, and policymakers who need to understand why a game is the right solution for their problem.
key_concepts: Portfolio construction: showcasing both game design skills and domain understanding with evidence of outcomes
Case study format for serious game portfolio: problem, stakeholders, design rationale, implementation, evaluation results, lessons learned
Industry landscape: edtech companies, health tech startups, defense contractors, training consultancies, non-profit organizations
Professional organizations: Games for Change, IGDA Serious Games SIG, Games for Health, Serious Games Association
Grant writing: translating game design into fundable research proposals for NSF, NIH, DoE, and private foundations
Stakeholder communication: explaining game design value to non-game audiences using their language and success metrics
Interdisciplinary skills: combining game design with education, psychology, health science, or business for career differentiation
Emerging opportunities: AI-powered adaptive learning games, VR training, digital therapeutics, gamified civic technology
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop: each student crafts a 2-minute pitch for their serious game prototype targeting a specific non-game audience (funder, school administrator, hospital director), followed by peer feedback on persuasiveness and clarity
setup_requirements: Portfolio template with serious game-specific sections
Grant opportunity database bookmarks
Pitch practice worksheet with audience-specific prompts
Professional network mapping tool

review.value: Serious game portfolios must include outcome data, not just screenshots and gameplay videos. Show that your game produced measurable learning gains, behavior change, or clinical improvement.
Funders want ROI and evidence. Clinicians want clinical outcomes and safety. Educators want learning standards alignment. Translate your game's value into the language each stakeholder speaks.
The most valuable serious game professionals have deep expertise in both game design and a domain field. A game designer who can read clinical trial methodology is rare and highly sought.
The leading organization and annual festival for social impact games. The G4C festival provides networking, awards recognition, and industry connections for serious game professionals.

class_schedule.topic: Final Project Presentations and Course Synthesis
learning_objectives: Present a completed serious game or gamification prototype with evaluation results
Provide constructive critique of peer work using evidence-based evaluation criteria
Synthesize course concepts into a personal design philosophy for purpose-driven games
Identify areas for continued professional development in serious games and gamification
readings: Review all course materials for comprehensive synthesis
Peer project documentation for critique preparation

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session is your opportunity to present your serious game or gamification prototype, share your evaluation results, and receive feedback from peers and the instructor. Each presentation follows the professional format we practiced: problem statement, design rationale, prototype demonstration, evaluation methodology and results, and lessons learned. Beyond the presentations, we synthesize fifteen weeks of learning into personal design philosophies. What do you believe about serious game design? What principles will guide your future work? Where do you draw ethical lines? How do you balance engagement with purpose, entertainment with evidence, creative vision with stakeholder requirements? These are the questions that define serious game designers as professionals. The field is growing rapidly as organizations across every sector recognize that engagement-driven design can improve outcomes in education, health, training, and social impact. The theoretical foundations, design skills, and evaluation methods you have developed in this course position you to contribute meaningfully to this growth -- whether as a designer, researcher, consultant, or entrepreneur.
key_concepts: Professional presentation format: problem, design, prototype, evaluation, lessons learned, future work
Constructive critique methodology: specific, evidence-based feedback focused on effectiveness rather than personal taste
Design philosophy articulation: your principles for balancing engagement and purpose in serious game design
Evidence synthesis: connecting your project results to the broader research literature on serious game effectiveness
Future of serious games: AI-adaptive learning, precision digital therapeutics, VR training at scale, gamified civic infrastructure
Ethical commitment: ongoing responsibility to design for player welfare, not just engagement metrics
Community engagement: staying connected to the serious games community through conferences, publications, and collaboration
Reflective practice: the habit of evaluating your own design decisions against evidence and principles
lecture_notes.demonstration: Student project presentations with structured peer critique and instructor feedback, followed by group synthesis discussion
setup_requirements: Final prototype build or comprehensive documentation
Evaluation data and analysis results
Presentation slides (10-15 slides)
Peer critique forms and course evaluation survey

review.value: A personal framework that articulates your principles for serious game design: when to prioritize engagement vs accuracy, how to handle ethical tensions, what evidence standard you hold yourself to, and what you believe games can uniquely accomplish.

The level of evidence you require before claiming your serious game is effective. At minimum: controlled comparison with appropriate measures. Ideally: published peer-reviewed results with effect sizes.
The ongoing habit of critically examining your own design decisions, evaluating outcomes honestly, and updating your approach based on evidence. The opposite of designing by assumption.

Serious games sit at the intersection of rapidly evolving fields. Staying current requires engaging with game design, learning science, behavioral psychology, and domain-specific research simultaneously.

quizzes.topic: Serious Games Foundations, Gamification Theory, and Educational Design

questions.question: A company implements a leaderboard for its sales team, and after an initial spike in performance, engagement drops significantly within two months. Using self-determination theory, explain why this happened and propose a redesign that addresses the underlying motivational issue.
questions.answer: The leaderboard relies on extrinsic motivation (competition and social comparison) which can undermine intrinsic motivation through the overjustification effect. It threatens autonomy (forced participation), reduces competence feelings for those consistently at the bottom (learned helplessness), and can damage relatedness through toxic competition. A redesign should incorporate personal progress tracking against own past performance (competence), optional participation and multiple paths to recognition (autonomy), and team-based collaborative challenges alongside individual goals (relatedness).

rubric.key: Identifies overjustification or extrinsic motivation problem using SDT
Correctly applies at least two SDT needs (autonomy, competence, relatedness)
Proposes concrete redesign addressing identified motivational issues

questions.question: Explain the difference between stealth assessment and bolted-on quizzes in educational game design. Why does stealth assessment typically produce better learning outcomes?
questions.answer: Stealth assessment measures learning through gameplay actions and decisions in real time, while bolted-on quizzes interrupt gameplay with explicit test questions. Stealth assessment produces better outcomes because: (1) it does not break flow state, maintaining the engagement that makes games effective for learning, (2) it measures applied knowledge (what players can DO) rather than declarative knowledge (what they can RECALL), which is closer to real-world competence, (3) it provides continuous data rather than point-in-time snapshots, allowing adaptive difficulty adjustment, and (4) it reduces test anxiety, which interferes with both performance and learning.

rubric.key: Clear distinction between stealth assessment and bolted-on quizzes
At least two valid reasons why stealth assessment improves outcomes
Connects to flow state or engagement preservation

questions.question: What is 'chocolate-covered broccoli' in serious game design? Give a specific example and explain how you would redesign it to integrate learning into the core mechanics.
questions.answer: Chocolate-covered broccoli describes serious games that wrap unengaging educational content in superficial game aesthetics without integrating learning into meaningful mechanics. Example: a math drill app where solving multiplication problems earns coins to buy decorations for a virtual house. The math and the decoration are completely disconnected -- the game does not need the math and the math does not need the game. Redesign: create a game where mathematical thinking IS the core mechanic. A city-building game where players must calculate resource ratios, optimize building placement using area calculations, and manage budgets through arithmetic -- making math the tool that enables gameplay success rather than a tax extracted before playing.

rubric.key: Accurate definition of chocolate-covered broccoli
Specific example with clear disconnect between game and learning
Redesign demonstrates genuine integration of learning into mechanics

quizzes.topic: Health Games, Corporate Training, and Social Impact

questions.question: A hospital wants to create a game to help children aged 8-12 manage Type 1 diabetes. Using Kirkpatrick's four levels, describe what you would measure at each level to determine if the game is effective. Include at least one specific metric per level.
questions.answer: Level 1 (Reaction): Player satisfaction surveys and session duration/voluntary replay rates measuring whether children enjoy playing. Level 2 (Learning): Pre/post knowledge tests on carbohydrate counting, insulin timing, and symptom recognition. Level 3 (Behavior): Self-reported blood glucose monitoring frequency and parental observation of self-management behaviors over 3 months. Level 4 (Results): Reduction in emergency room visits for diabetic episodes and improvement in HbA1c levels over 6 months.

rubric.key: Correctly names and applies all four Kirkpatrick levels
Provides specific, measurable metrics for each level
Metrics are clinically appropriate for pediatric diabetes context

questions.question: Describe three specific accessibility features you would implement in a serious game for corporate emergency evacuation training, and explain how each ensures usability across different disabilities without reducing training effectiveness.
questions.answer: 1) Multiple input modes: keyboard-only navigation, switch controls, and voice commands alongside mouse/touch, ensuring motor-impaired employees can complete all scenarios. Route decisions remain meaningful regardless of input method. 2) Audio descriptions with haptic feedback: narrated environmental hazard descriptions and vibration cues for proximity to dangers, enabling visually impaired employees to learn spatial evacuation awareness through alternative senses. 3) Adjustable cognitive load: simplified UI with reduced simultaneous information, extended time limits, and step-by-step guided mode for cognitive disabilities, allowing all employees to learn the same procedures at an appropriate pace without skipping training content.

rubric.key: Three features addressing different disability categories
Clear explanation of how each accommodates specific disabilities
Demonstrates that training effectiveness is preserved

questions.question: Explain procedural rhetoric as defined by Ian Bogost and give one example of a game that uses procedural rhetoric to make a social argument. Identify the specific mechanic that creates the argument.
questions.answer: Procedural rhetoric is the practice of making arguments through the rules and mechanics of a game rather than through narrative, dialogue, or visual content. The game's systems embody the argument. Example: Papers, Please makes an argument about the moral complexity of immigration enforcement. The mechanic: players must check documents against increasingly complex and contradictory rules under time pressure. The rules force players to choose between following unjust orders (accepting financial reward) and helping refugees (risking punishment and family starvation). The procedural argument is that bureaucratic systems create moral dilemmas that cannot be resolved without personal cost.

rubric.key: Accurate definition of procedural rhetoric
Valid game example with social argument identified
Specific mechanic that creates the argument clearly explained

quizzes.topic: Behavior Change, Accessibility, and Persuasive Design

questions.question: Using Fogg's B=MAP model, explain why a well-designed fitness gamification app might fail to change exercise behavior for some users. Identify which component is most likely to be the bottleneck and propose a design solution.
questions.answer: Fogg's model states Behavior = Motivation + Ability + Prompt, all present simultaneously. A fitness app might provide strong Motivation (rewards, social competition, progress tracking) and Prompts (push notifications), but fail on Ability for users who face real barriers: no gym access, physical limitations, time constraints, or lack of exercise knowledge. Ability is most likely the bottleneck because gamification typically addresses motivation but assumes users can already perform the behavior. Design solution: integrate Ability scaffolding -- offer 5-minute micro-workouts that require no equipment for users with time/access constraints, video tutorials for exercise form, and adaptive difficulty that starts from the user's actual fitness level rather than assuming baseline capability.

rubric.key: Correctly applies all three B=MAP components to the scenario
Identifies Ability as the likely bottleneck with specific barriers
Proposes design solution that addresses the identified bottleneck

questions.question: A gamification system for student homework completion uses a 30-day streak mechanic. Students who miss one day lose their entire streak and must restart from zero. Analyze this design using self-determination theory and the Transtheoretical model, and propose a specific improvement.
questions.answer: SDT analysis: the all-or-nothing streak undermines Competence (one missed day erases weeks of achievement, creating helplessness), Autonomy (forces daily compliance with no flexibility), and Relatedness (isolated individual tracking with no social support for recovery). Transtheoretical analysis: the harsh reset punishes relapse so severely that students in the Action stage who experience a setback are pushed back to Precontemplation ('this is impossible, why bother') rather than supported through recovery. Improvement: replace with a 'heat map' system that shows consistency over time rather than consecutive days. Allow 2 'grace days' per month that do not break the pattern. Add a 'comeback' mechanic that rewards returning after a gap rather than punishing the gap. This supports relapse recovery while still incentivizing consistency.

rubric.key: Identifies specific SDT needs undermined by the streak design
Applies Transtheoretical model to explain relapse impact
Proposes specific improvement addressing both frameworks

questions.question: What is the difference between persuasion and manipulation in serious game design? Give one example of a game mechanic that persuades ethically and one that crosses into manipulation.
questions.answer: Persuasion presents an argument or experience that the player can critically evaluate and freely accept or reject. Manipulation bypasses rational evaluation by exploiting psychological vulnerabilities. Ethical persuasion: a climate change game that simulates the long-term consequences of policy choices, allowing players to see outcomes and draw their own conclusions. The game presents evidence through experience; the player retains agency. Manipulation: a charity donation game that shows increasingly graphic images of suffering children with a countdown timer, exploiting guilt and urgency to pressure immediate donations. The mechanic bypasses rational evaluation of the charity's effectiveness by triggering emotional distress.

rubric.key: Clear distinction between persuasion and manipulation
Valid example of ethical persuasion through game mechanics
Valid example of manipulation with explanation of exploitation

quizzes.topic: Research Methods, Civic Games, and Analytics

questions.question: You are evaluating an educational serious game. Your randomized controlled trial shows a statistically significant improvement (p = 0.03) with an effect size of Cohen's d = 0.15. Should you report this game as effective? Explain your reasoning.
questions.answer: Despite statistical significance, the game should not be reported as effective in practical terms. A Cohen's d of 0.15 is a trivially small effect size (below the 0.20 threshold for 'small'). The statistical significance likely resulted from a large sample size that made even tiny differences detectable. In practical terms, a d of 0.15 means the game produced a barely perceptible improvement that would not be noticeable in real-world educational outcomes. The report should honestly state: the result is statistically significant but practically negligible, and the game does not produce meaningful educational improvement over the control condition. This is why effect size reporting is essential alongside p-values.

rubric.key: Correctly interprets d = 0.15 as practically negligible
Explains relationship between sample size and statistical significance
Articulates why effect size matters more than p-value for practical decisions

questions.question: A gamification system for a corporate wellness program shows that 85% of employees earned the maximum points in the first month. The wellness team celebrates this as a success. As the analytics consultant, what concerns would you raise and what data would you examine?
questions.answer: Concerns: (1) 85% maximum completion may indicate gaming the system rather than genuine wellness behavior -- employees may be checking boxes without actually changing habits. (2) The points threshold may be too easy, making maximum points achievable without meaningful behavior change. (3) The novelty effect means month 1 data is unreliable for predicting sustained engagement. Data to examine: (1) Compare point-earning patterns with actual wellness outcomes (health metrics, gym usage, step counts from fitness trackers) to verify behavior change. (2) Look at the distribution of point-earning -- is it evenly spread or concentrated in last-minute spikes? (3) Track month 2-3 engagement to see if the pattern sustains or shows the classic novelty decline. (4) Survey employees qualitatively about whether the program changed their actual habits.

rubric.key: Identifies gaming-the-system or compliance concern
Raises novelty effect or sustainability concern
Proposes specific data examinations to validate or refute the claim

questions.question: Name two risks specific to gamifying civic engagement (such as participatory budgeting) that do not apply to gamifying corporate training. Explain why each risk exists.
questions.answer: Risk 1: Gaming by organized interests. In corporate training, all employees have roughly equal incentive to participate. In civic gamification, organized political groups can coordinate to dominate the system, drowning out individual citizen voices. A gamified participatory budget could be captured by a well-organized faction that earns maximum points and steers funds to their preferred projects. Risk 2: Democratic trivialization. Corporate training gamification makes a mundane task more engaging, which is benign. Civic gamification risks reducing democratic participation (voting, deliberation, policy input) to a game, potentially undermining the seriousness and gravitas that democratic decisions require. Citizens may treat consequential decisions casually if they feel like gameplay rather than governance.

rubric.key: Two distinct risks specific to civic context (not applicable to corporate)
Clear explanation of why each risk is unique to civic engagement

quizzes.topic: Development Process, Emerging Technology, and Professional Practice

questions.question: A VR surgical training simulation costs 5x more to develop than an equivalent desktop version. Under what specific conditions would the VR investment be justified? Under what conditions would the desktop version be the better choice?
questions.answer: VR justified when: (1) The training requires spatial understanding and hand-eye coordination that desktop cannot adequately simulate (e.g., laparoscopic surgery where 3D depth perception and bimanual dexterity are core skills). (2) Controlled studies demonstrate measurably better transfer to real surgical performance from VR versus desktop. (3) The training population is large enough that the higher development cost amortizes to acceptable per-trainee cost. (4) The alternative (practicing on cadavers or real patients) is more expensive or higher risk than VR. Desktop better when: (1) The training primarily involves decision-making, protocol knowledge, or diagnostic reasoning that does not require spatial manipulation. (2) The learner population is distributed globally and cannot access VR hardware. (3) Preliminary studies show equivalent outcomes on desktop. (4) Budget constraints mean VR development would come at the expense of broader content coverage.

rubric.key: At least two specific conditions justifying VR with reasoning
At least two specific conditions favoring desktop with reasoning
Reasoning connects to evidence-based criteria rather than assumptions

questions.question: You are writing a grant proposal for an educational serious game. The review panel includes educators and government officials with no game design background. Describe how you would explain the value proposition of a game-based approach without using game design jargon.
questions.answer: Frame the pitch around outcomes they care about in their language: 'Our interactive learning tool achieves 3x higher completion rates than traditional e-learning modules because it motivates students through meaningful practice rather than passive consumption. Students apply knowledge to solve realistic problems with immediate feedback on their reasoning, not just their answers. The system continuously adapts difficulty to each student's skill level, keeping every learner challenged but not frustrated. Built-in assessment tracks what each student can actually DO, not just what they can recall on a test. Preliminary testing with 200 students showed a 40% improvement in practical skill application compared to the standard curriculum, measured by independent evaluators using the district's existing assessment framework.'

rubric.key: Explains game-based value without game design jargon
Frames benefits in educator/funder language (outcomes, evidence, standards)
Includes specific, quantifiable claims that a review panel would find credible
