questions.question: What is the most important difference between building a portfolio for entertainment game design and building a portfolio for serious game design? What must a serious game portfolio include that an entertainment portfolio does not?
questions.answer: The most important difference is that a serious game portfolio must include evidence of outcomes, not just evidence of design quality. An entertainment game portfolio shows that you can make fun, polished, engaging games. A serious game portfolio must show that your games produce measurable results -- learning gains, behavior changes, clinical improvements, training transfer. This means including: evaluation data (pre/post test results, comparison with control groups, effect sizes), user research findings (not just usability but effectiveness), and documentation of how game mechanics connect to the serious purpose with evidence that the connection works. An entertainment portfolio succeeds by showing craft. A serious game portfolio succeeds by showing impact.

rubric.key: Identifies evidence of outcomes as the key differentiator
Specifies what evidence must be included (data, evaluation, impact measurement)
Contrasts with entertainment portfolio requirements clearly

major_assignments.title: Gamification System Design and Analysis
major_assignments.description: Select a real-world non-game context (workplace, education, health, civic engagement) and design a comprehensive gamification system. Conduct a full Octalysis analysis, apply self-determination theory to justify design decisions, evaluate an existing gamification implementation in the same domain, and produce a professional design document with user journey mapping.
objectives: Apply the Octalysis framework and SDT to design a gamification system grounded in behavioral psychology
Demonstrate understanding of intrinsic vs extrinsic motivation and the overjustification effect in system design
Critically evaluate an existing gamification implementation using evidence-based criteria
Produce professional design documentation suitable for stakeholder presentation and developer handoff
deliverables: Gamification design document with complete Octalysis analysis and SDT justification (8-10 pages)
Comparative evaluation of an existing system in the same domain with evidence-based critique (3-4 pages)
User journey map showing player progression through the gamified system across behavior change stages
Presentation slides summarizing the design rationale with speaker notes (10-15 slides)
Analytics plan specifying what metrics to track and how to evaluate system effectiveness

criteria: Gamification mechanics are well-suited to the chosen domain and target population
All eight Octalysis core drives are addressed with clear justification for emphasis and de-emphasis
Design avoids common anti-patterns (pointsification, chocolate-covered broccoli, exploitative mechanics)
User progression is well-paced, motivationally sustainable, and aligned with behavior change stages
Self-determination theory correctly applied to specific design decisions with citations
Intrinsic and extrinsic motivation balanced with overjustification risks explicitly addressed
Behavioral psychology principles cited with academic sources supporting mechanic choices
Long-term sustainability addressed with plan for maintaining engagement beyond novelty period
Existing system evaluated using consistent, evidence-based criteria
Strengths and weaknesses identified with specific supporting evidence
Clear articulation of how proposed design improves on existing approaches
Fair and balanced assessment that acknowledges competitor strengths
Professional writing quality with proper academic citation format
Clear visual diagrams, user journey maps, and Octalysis radar charts
Effective presentation slides suitable for non-game stakeholders
Analytics plan is specific, measurable, and connected to design objectives
Comprehensive gamification design with strong theoretical foundation in SDT and Octalysis
Evidence-based comparative analysis with actionable conclusions
Professional documentation suitable for industry stakeholder presentation
Analytics plan demonstrating measurement-oriented design thinking

major_assignments.title: Serious Game Prototype with Embedded Assessment
major_assignments.description: Design and develop a playable serious game prototype addressing a real-world problem in education, health, training, or social impact. The game must demonstrate measurable learning or behavioral objectives with embedded assessment, include documented accessibility features, and be validated through structured playtesting with at least 5 participants.
objectives: Create a functional serious game prototype with clearly defined and measurable objectives
Implement embedded assessment mechanics that measure player progress toward objectives during gameplay
Apply accessibility guidelines to ensure inclusive design across multiple disability dimensions
Conduct structured playtesting and iterate based on evidence with documented before/after changes
deliverables: Playable prototype (digital or tabletop with complete rules and materials)
Design document: target audience, objectives, mechanics justification, embedded assessment specification (8-10 pages)
Accessibility audit documenting accommodations implemented across motor, visual, auditory, and cognitive dimensions
Playtesting report with methodology, participant demographics, data collected, findings, and documented iterations
Evaluation plan for a full-scale study including research design, measures, sample size calculation, and timeline

criteria: Clear, measurable alignment between game mechanics and learning/behavioral objectives
Embedded assessment meaningfully measures player progress without breaking engagement flow
Game maintains genuine engagement while delivering on its serious purpose (avoids chocolate-covered broccoli)
Target audience and context are well-defined with design decisions justified for that population
Prototype is functional and playable without critical bugs or confusing interactions
Core mechanics are fully implemented and demonstrate the design intent
Assessment data is collected and accessible for analysis in structured format
Build runs on intended platform without errors or dependency issues
At least three accessibility features implemented across different disability dimensions
Design considers diverse abilities from the outset rather than adding accommodations as afterthought
Accommodations maintain the effectiveness of the serious purpose (not bypassing learning/therapy)
Audit checklist completed with honest assessment of gaps and future improvement priorities
Playtesting conducted with at least 5 appropriate participants using structured methodology
Data collected systematically with clear measures aligned to objectives
Iterations documented with specific before/after comparisons showing evidence-based changes
Full evaluation plan is methodologically sound with appropriate research design and realistic timeline
Prototype effectively addresses a real-world problem through integrated game design
Embedded assessment demonstrates measurable progress toward defined objectives
Inclusive design with documented accessibility features across disability dimensions
Evidence-based iteration from structured playtesting with methodologically sound evaluation plan

major_assignments.title: Serious Games Industry Analysis and Design Philosophy
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive analysis of a serious games domain (education, health, corporate training, social impact, or civic engagement), evaluating the current landscape of published games, research evidence, market trends, and emerging opportunities. Synthesize your analysis with a personal design philosophy document that articulates your principles for creating effective, ethical serious games.
objectives: Demonstrate comprehensive understanding of a serious games domain through rigorous analysis
Evaluate the evidence base for serious game effectiveness in the chosen domain
Identify market gaps and emerging opportunities for impactful serious game development
Articulate a personal design philosophy grounded in course theory and practical experience
deliverables: Domain analysis report: landscape survey, evidence review, market analysis, opportunity identification (10-12 pages)
Annotated bibliography of 15+ academic sources on serious game effectiveness in the domain
Design philosophy document articulating personal principles for serious game design (3-4 pages)
Concept proposal for a new serious game addressing an identified opportunity (2-3 pages with wireframes)
Reflective portfolio connecting all course projects to the articulated design philosophy

criteria: Comprehensive landscape survey covering major published serious games in the domain
Evidence review evaluates research quality, not just findings (distinguishes strong from weak evidence)
Market analysis identifies stakeholders, funding sources, and competitive landscape
Emerging opportunities are specific, justified by evidence gaps, and feasible to pursue
Annotated bibliography covers diverse sources (journal articles, conference papers, industry reports)
Each annotation evaluates methodology quality, not just summarizes findings
Synthesis identifies patterns, contradictions, and gaps in the evidence base
Critical perspective on publication bias and evidence limitations
Philosophy articulates clear principles for balancing engagement with serious purpose
Ethical commitments are specific and connected to course frameworks (SDT, accessibility, persuasion ethics)
Principles are grounded in both theory and personal experience from course projects
Philosophy addresses how to handle common tensions (stakeholder vs user needs, engagement vs accuracy)
Proposal addresses a specific opportunity identified in the domain analysis
Design decisions are justified by the articulated design philosophy
Wireframes communicate the concept clearly and demonstrate integrated mechanic-purpose alignment
Feasibility is addressed with realistic scope, timeline, and funding strategy
Rigorous domain analysis demonstrating comprehensive understanding of serious games landscape
Critical evidence evaluation showing research literacy and analytical sophistication
Authentic design philosophy grounded in theory and personal experience
Feasible concept proposal demonstrating ability to translate analysis into actionable design

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes assessing theoretical knowledge in gamification, serious game design, evaluation methods, and ethical frameworks

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on exercises in gamification design, game analysis, accessibility auditing, and playtesting methodology

major_assignments.description: Three comprehensive projects: gamification system design, serious game prototype, and industry analysis with design philosophy

midterm_examination.description: Mixed format exam covering serious game theory, gamification frameworks, evaluation methods, and ethical analysis

final_project.description: Capstone industry analysis and design philosophy demonstrating integrated course competency

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all playtesting sessions, peer critiques, and guest speaker events. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Playtesting deadlines are firm due to scheduled participant sessions and cannot be extended.
course_policies.collaboration: Individual assignments must be completed independently. The serious game prototype may be completed individually or in teams of up to 3, with peer evaluation required for team submissions.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All designs and written work must be original. External game assets must be properly licensed and attributed. Plagiarism of game mechanics documentation will be treated as academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need access to a game development tool of their choice (Unity, Godot, Twine, tabletop prototyping materials). Free options are acceptable. All playtesting sessions require a functional build or prototype.
